instructor.name: Richard M. Feldman
instructor.office_hours: Monday, Wednesday, 10AM to 11AM or by appointment Monday through Saturday by Zoom

course_metadata.meeting_times: Lecture: Monday, Wednesday, 9:10 AM - 10:00 AM; Lab: Friday, 9:10 AM - 11:40 AM for the lab (9:10 AM - 9:15 AM is Attendance Quiz)

chunks.title: Syllabus for ISEN 355.501 -- Spring 2026
chunks.content: The content contained in this document may change before January 12, 2026, and only becomes the official syllabus on the first day of class. A copy of this syllabus will be posted in Howdy, but the official course syllabus is the one found in Canvas.

chunks.title: Course Description
chunks.content: Systems simulation structure, logic and methodologies; development of simulation models; data handling methods; analysis of simulation data; verification and validation; system simulation languages, model and analysis; application to industrial situations.

chunks.title: Communication
chunks.content:
My hearing is very bad so do not be surprised if you are asked to repeat questions raised in class. Please persevere in asking questions despite having to repeat questions. It is more enjoyable to teach with questions.

The main mode of communication outside of the classroom will be by email and Zoom sessions. To avoid my spam filter, it is important for you to use your TAMU email address. I am available six days a week, not on Sunday. Please use your video while in a Zoom session. Using your video helps with my hearing; however, be prepared to use Chat to further assist in communication.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
• Develop models using commercially available discrete event (process-oriented) simulation software,
• Interpret simulation output using valid statistical methods,
• Collect and analyze input data using valid statistical methods, and
• Apply simulation to model industrial and system engineering problems.

chunks.title: ABET Outcome Assessed in this Course
chunks.content: An ability to develop and conduct appropriate experimentation, analyze and interpret data, and use engineering judgment to draw conclusions. (ABET Outcome 6).

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:
Jerry Banks, John Carson, Barry Nelson, David Nicol (2010). Discrete-Event System Simulation (5th Edition), Prentice Hall.

Recommended. A.M. Law (2024). Simulation Modeling and Analysis, 6th Edition. McGraw Hill Publishing.

Free On-line Textbook. Jeffrey S. Smith and David T. Sturrock (2024). Simio and Simulation -- Modeling, Analysis, Applications, 7th Edition. (https://textbook.simio.com/SASMAA7).

Free On-line Workbook. Jeffrey A. Joines and Michael E. Kuhl (2024). Simulation Modeling with SIMIO: A Workbook, 6th Edition. (https://textbook.simio.com/Workbook6)

chunks.title: Software
chunks.content:
Simio
The Industrial and Systems Engineering Department uses Simio, a fourth generation software system, under a grant from Simio LLC (https://www.simio.com). A copy of the Simio software is installed on departmental machines, but you should also install the software on your personal machine. A complimentary professional version of Simio is available for all students registered in the course and is obtained by filling out an application found at this Simio website: https://forms.simio.com/f/student-license. When filling out the application, you must supply a Student License Claim Code. The code can be found under the Monday page of the Week 1 Canvas Module or go to the Canvas home page for this course, click on the icon labelled "Simulation Resources", and see paragraph 1.1 under "Resources for Getting Started".

Excel
We will frequently use Excel during class time to illustrate various statistical and simulation concepts so familiarity with Excel is expected. Several homework problems will also involve Excel and it may be required for tests.

3D Warehouse
Before mid-February, go to https://3dwarehouse.sketchup.com/ and create an account. During week 7, we will discuss animation which will include downloading 3D images from the 3D Warehouse. An account (which is free) is required for the download.

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
• Grades assigned are A for 90%-100%, B for 80%–89.9%, C for 70%–79.9%, D for 60%-
69.9% and F for less than 60%. For students opting out of the final exam, grades are truncated; otherwise, they are rounded; thus, 89.99% is a B if the final exam is not taken. Other than rounding your grade for those taking the final, there will likely be no curve at the end of the semester. Remember, grades are based on performance over graded material, and not on what you need, want, or how hard you worked. There will be no “extra” assignments or projects available for improving your grade beyond what is described in this section and is required of all students.
• Grades will be assigned to homework (usually 5 points but once or twice 10 points), lab assignments (8 or sometimes 10 points), attendance quizzes (2 points), two lecture tests (100 points each), two lab tests (100 points each), and an optional final exam (100 points). Attendance quizzes will be given during the first 5 minutes of most labs. Homework will be assigned at the end of most lectures and will usually be due by midnight immediately before the next class period. There may be one or two weeks in which there are two homework assignments given at the end of class with the possibility that a homework assignment will have a due date beyond the next day. There is also the possibility that an assignment is given during the class period that must be finished by the end of the class period. Note that class attendance is required, not optional.
• For students who opt to not take the final, the weights for the grading components are as follows: each lab test=24%, each lecture test=20%, the sum of all grades for homework, labs, and attendance quizzes=12%.
• For students who do take the final, the weights for the grading components are as follows: each lab test=19.2%, each lecture test=16%, the sum of all grades for homework, labs, and attendance quizzes=9.6%, and the final exam=20%. In addition, there will be a grade replacement policy for students taking the final, namely, the lowest grade among the five test grades will be replaced by the second lowest grade.
• Note that if you opt to take the final and wish to change the date because you have two other finals on the same day, you must notify me of your intent by April16 (see Student Rule 8.4 (https://student-rules.tamu.edu/rule08) ) by sending me (via email attachment) your completed form (http://us.tamu.edu/Students/Final-Exam-Conflict)_for rescheduling a final exam.
• The best practice for maximizing your grade is to make sure you understand Monday's homework at the end of each week and re-work the lab assignment from scratch the day after turning it in. You cannot “cram” for lab exams.

chunks.title: Statement on the Use of Artificial Intelligence
chunks.content: The use of artificial intelligence (AI) on tests and for lab assignments is prohibited. The use of AI homework is not recommended but is acceptable if it helps in learning the material.

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
• Assignments are not accepted after their due date.
• Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempted from the late work policy (Student Rule 7 (https://student-rules.tamu.edu/rule07) ). However, if the excused absence is not due to an unexpected event, I must be notified ahead of time by email using your TAMU email address; otherwise, an extension to the deadline for turning in the assignment will not be given.

chunks.title: Attendance Policy (Course-Specific)
chunks.content: Class attendance is required. If a student misses class, it is the student's responsibility to find out what was missed during the class period.

chunks.title: Course Schedule
chunks.content:
Week 1 (1/12)
Introduction to simulation and its relationship with probability and statistics.
Lab 1
Simio User Interface: Driver's License Office.
Week 2 (1/19) No Monday class. Wed: Output analysis, confidence intervals, and probability distributions.
Lab 2
Simio help, branching, time in the system.
Week 3 (1/26) Non-stationary arrival process introduction.
Lab 3
Model entity state variables, model entity properties, feedback loops.
Week 4 (2/2)
Input analysis and goodness of fit tests.
Lab 4
Work in progress, capacity, plots and pie charts.
Week 5 (2/9)
Distribution fitting and parameter estimation using statistical methods.
Lab 5
Model entity state variables and properties, node list, tally statistic.
Week 6 (2/16)
Random number generation. Wed: Random variate generation.
Lab 6
Model state variables, tally, output, state statistics.
Week 7 (2/23)
Lecture test tentatively on Feb 23. (Makeup exam, 2/27 during lab.) Wed:
Introduce sequences/routing.
Lab 7
Animation in Simio.
Week 8 (3/2)
Statistical analysis, comparing systems.
Lab 8
Lab test tentatively on March 6. (Makeup exam, 3/20 during lab.)
Week 9: SPRING BREAK: March 9 to 13
Week 10 (3/16) Non-stationary arrival processes.
Lab 10
Simio processes including stations.
Week 11 (3/23)
Simio and Excel.
Lab 11
Arrival tables, Resources.
Week 12 (3/30) Combiners and separators.
Lab 12
Good Friday. No Lab.
Week 13 (4/6)
Transporters.
Lab 13
Sequences.
Week 14 (4/13)
Lecture test tentatively on April 13. (Makeup exam, 4/17 during lab.) Wed:
Verification and validation.
Lab 14
Combiners and Separators.
Week 15 (4/20) Optional simulation topic.
Lab 15
Lab test tentatively on April 24. (Makeup exam, 4/28 during lab.)
Week 16 (4/27)
Semester wrap-up.
Lab 16
Meets on Tuesday, Redefined day. Cost and Profit.
Final Exam
Optional final on Friday, May 1, from 8 AM to 10 AM.
(If you plan to take the final, please let me know by email before 5PM
Wednesday, 4/29.)

chunks.title: Academic Integrity Statement and Policy (Course-Specific Enforcement)
chunks.content:
The Industrial and Systems Engineering Department has adopted a “no tolerance” position with respect to violations of the Aggie Honor Code. In this course, I will request the Aggie Honor Office to assign a grade of F* for any violation of the Aggie Honor Code, no matter how minimal the violation may be.

For this course, it is permissible to discuss and compare lab and homework assignments with other students. Some Canvas Quizzes have passwords needed to access the quiz. This happens when the quiz must be taken during class; therefore, it is considered academically dishonest to share a quiz password with any student not physically present in the classroom.

When access to a computer is required for a test, it is academically dishonest and a violation of the Aggie Honor Code to access a search engine or attempt to use artificial intelligence during the test.
